topic.title: Bayes' Rule
topic.question: How does Bayes' rule update beliefs given evidence, and why is the posterior proportional to likelihood times prior?

promptVariant.name: Whiteboard prompt v4 - board state coherence check

lesson.title: Bayes' Rule: Updating Beliefs with Evidence
lesson.payoff: A disease with a 1-in-1000 base rate can jump to over 90% probability after one positive test -- Bayes' rule is the exact formula that computes that 900x shift, and it underlies spam filters, medical diagnosis, and search ranking.
staircase: likelihood of evidence under each hypothesis
total probability of evidence across all hypotheses
posterior proportional to likelihood times prior
lesson.destination: You will hold the formula P(H|E) = P(E|H) * P(H) / P(E), understand that P(E) is a normalizing constant summing over all hypotheses, and see why the posterior is proportional to likelihood times prior.

narration.fullText: Suppose a disease affects one person in a thousand. You take a test and it comes back positive. How much should that change your belief that you have the disease? That question is exactly what Bayes' rule answers. Let me draw a probability tree -- a branching diagram where each branch represents a hypothesis and each leaf represents an outcome -- to map out the possibilities. The root splits into H, meaning you have the disease, and not-H, meaning you do not. Each branch carries a prior probability -- your degree of belief before seeing any evidence. The prior P(H) is one in a thousand, so P(not-H) is 999 in a thousand. Now for each branch we attach a likelihood -- the probability of the test result E, a positive test, given that hypothesis. P(E|H), the probability of a positive test given disease, is 0.99. P(E|not-H), the probability of a positive test without disease -- the false-positive rate -- is 0.02. To apply Bayes' rule we need P(E), the total probability of a positive test regardless of disease status, which we compute by summing over both branches: P(E) equals P(E|H) times P(H) plus P(E|not-H) times P(not-H). This is the law of total probability -- it weights each branch's likelihood by how probable that branch is. Now I can write Bayes' rule: P(H|E) equals P(E|H) times P(H), all divided by P(E). The numerator is one branch's contribution; the denominator normalizes so the result is a valid probability. That is why the posterior -- your belief after evidence -- is proportional to likelihood times prior: the denominator is a fixed constant once E is observed. Let me fill in the numbers: P(E|H) times P(H) equals 0.99 times 0.001 which is 0.00099. P(E|not-H) times P(not-H) equals 0.02 times 0.999 which is 0.01998. So P(E) equals 0.02097. P(H|E) equals 0.00099 divided by 0.02097, which is about 0.047 -- roughly five percent. That 900x jump from 0.1% to 4.7% is real, but a single imperfect test still leaves you mostly uninfected. The same formula works for any H and any E: posterior is always proportional to likelihood times prior, normalized by total evidence.

actions.content: P( disease | positive test ) = ?

A probability tree rooted at a circle labeled Root on the left; two branches extending right: upper branch labeled H (disease), lower branch labeled not-H (no disease); each branch ends in a node from which two leaves extend -- upper-upper leaf labeled E (positive), upper-lower leaf labeled not-E; lower-upper leaf labeled E (positive), lower-lower leaf labeled not-E; branch and leaf edges are unlabeled placeholders awaiting probabilities
P(H) = prior -- belief before evidence
trace the upper branch H in blue and write placeholder P(H) beside it, trace the lower branch not-H in blue and write placeholder P(not-H) beside it
P(E|H) = likelihood -- prob of evidence given hypothesis

A horizontal bar spanning full width divided into two segments: left segment colored blue labeled H branch: P(E|H)*P(H) with width proportional to 0.00099, right segment colored orange labeled not-H branch: P(E|not-H)*P(not-H) with width proportional to 0.01998; a bracket above the full bar labeled P(E)
Label the upper branch H with P(H)=0.001, lower branch not-H with P(not-H)=0.999; label upper-upper leaf edge with P(E|H)=0.99 and lower-upper leaf edge with P(E|not-H)=0.02
P(E) = P(E|H)*P(H) + P(E|not-H)*P(not-H)
all
P(H|E) = [ P(E|H) * P(H) ] / P(E)
box the numerator P(E|H)*P(H) in red to show it is the single-branch contribution
underline the denominator P(E) in green and write normalizing constant beside it
Write the proportionality statement as a short equation: P(H|E) proportional-to P(E|H) * P(H), with a double-headed arrow pointing to the boxed numerator in the Bayes formula above
draw a vertical red arrow pointing to the tiny blue left segment and label it posterior mass to show how small P(H|E) remains relative to P(E)
circle the entire formula in gold and write posterior proportional to likelihood times prior directly below it
Works for any H, any E -- same structure every time
all
